message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs. It includes 3,044 annotated sentences in 100 dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates on-task and off-task intents. On-task intents are defined specifically for each task, while off-task intents use universal dialog-act categories consisting of six general intents and six social intents. Each conversation turn is segmented into single sentences, and annotation is done at the sentence level rather than the turn level.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP). RIP and RSP compare predicted response intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP allow multiple acceptable system intents by scoring exact matches as 1 and otherwise using the transition probability \(p(I_{predict}\mid I_i)\) estimated from training-data human-intent/system-intent bigrams. Human evaluation uses Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length, and Task Success Score, where task success is the average types of attacker information obtained (name, address, phone number).

The paper reports F1 scores rather than a single accuracy metric. Compared to other systems, OpenTapioca scores lower on AIDA-CoNLL (0.482 micro / 0.399 macro vs AIDA 0.725 / 0.684) and RSS-500 (0.335 / 0.310 vs AIDA 0.455 / 0.447), but higher on ISTEX-1000 (0.870 / 0.858 vs the best baseline DBP Spotlight at 0.574 / 0.575) and Microposts 2016 in micro F1 (0.087 vs AIDA 0.056).

They compare against two state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines: Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012). Both use message-, user-, topic-, and propagation-based features with an SVM classifier.

They report accuracy and also use the standard TDT evaluation procedure with official TDT3 scripts, which evaluates detection using Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves. They also measure efficiency by throughput per second.
Yes. They rely on classification using an SVM, and the paper states that all training and optimization use the training set, with performance reported on a separate test set.
Yes. They created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo’s official rumour debunking service and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese.

They define rumours as unconfirmed, novel information with respect to news articles. In their task formulation, a message is predicted to be a rumour if it is likely to become a future rumour at the time of publication.
LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
Generative document evaluation and document classification.
CoNLL2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 for English; OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese part), MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER for Chinese.

They incorporate direction and relative distance by replacing vanilla self-attention with relative positional attention: they define a relative positional encoding \(R_{t-j}\) from the offset \(t-j\) and compute attention as \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_{t-j}\) depends on the signed offset, with sine terms changing sign for forward vs. backward positions while cosine terms stay the same, the attention can distinguish both direction and distance.

Yes. The paper states that TENER achieves superior performance over prevailing BiLSTM-based models, and on English NER it reports new state-of-the-art results on two datasets under the non-contextualized embedding setting.
The state-of-the-art systems are SEMAFOR, SLING, Stanford KBP, and the state-of-the-art machine learning approach on the MetaQA dataset [20].

They evaluated KALM-QA on two datasets: a manually constructed general-question dataset based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. For knowledge authoring, they evaluated on 250 manually constructed sentences adapted from FrameNet exemplar sentences.

The paper contributes: (i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation of the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. The pre-trained models and scripts to run or retrain the system are also publicly available.

The paper uses the best system from EVENTI Subtask B, FBK-HLT (Mirza and Minard, 2014), as the baseline. FBK-HLT is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.
Unanswerable

They assign a cluster’s semantic concept label by first disambiguating each word in the cluster to WordNet synsets, choosing the set of synsets that minimizes the total distance between them because the clustered words are assumed to be semantically close in the domain. They then generate candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those chosen synsets and rank the candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the chosen synsets, selecting labels with the least total distance.

They discover coherent word clusters by training domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings and applying k-means to the embeddings of gender-associated words, running k-means 50 times and keeping the solution with the lowest sum of squared errors. They then use WordNet-based sense disambiguation and hypernym-based label generation/ranking to interpret the clusters, and report that the resulting clusters are coherent in context.
The two introduced datasets contain 15,917 celebrity news articles and 283,973 RateMyProfessors reviews.
The authors used the centroid of the cluster as a strong baseline label. They also compare word intrusion performance against a random baseline of 20%.
They generally rank low in appropriateness. Data-driven systems often produce flirtatious responses, and only Neuralconvo and Cleverbot ever politely refuse.
They collected a total of 2441 system replies via this crowdsourcing-based study. They also summarised the source data into 109 prototypical abusive utterances.
Yes. The automatic annotation of time expressions was evaluated: the customised HeidelTime version used for it achieves an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional. Czech is also treated as a separate type/cluster alongside the agglutinative languages.

Yes. The morphology-level model uses gold morphological analysis consisting of the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, and the conclusion explicitly mentions rich derivational morphology and tokens with high numbers of morphological tags, indicating consideration of both derivational and inflectional morphology.

Gold morphological features are used as outputs of the morphology-level model. For all languages, this consists of the token’s lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, with additional information for some languages such as part-of-speech tags for Turkish.

The paper uses the Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO benchmarks.
English

The paper explores two ad-hoc approaches as baselines: a direct combination baseline that computes figure-caption correspondence through the scalar product between the softmax outputs of separately trained vision and language networks, and a supervised pre-training baseline that freezes the feature extraction trunks of those networks, assembles them into the FCC architecture, and trains only the fully connected layers. In TQA, the authors also mention adapting the VQA baseline to fit their setting.
They compared with the supervised TQA baselines from Kembhavi et al. (2017): random, BiDAF, text only (TQA1, based on MemoryNet), text+image (TQA2, VQA), and text+diagrams (TQA3, DSDP-NET).

Yes. The main datasets used are scientific publications corpora such as Semantic Scholar and Springer Nature’s SciGraph, and the TQA corpus is from middle school science curricula, although Wikipedia is noted as a general-purpose source.

Their figures and captions come from scientific publications in the Semantic Scholar corpus, where PDFs were downloaded and 12.5M figures and captions were extracted using PDFFigures2. They also used a smaller set from the intersection of Springer Nature’s SciGraph with Semantic Scholar, yielding 82K figures.
Unanswerable
No. The paper states that their model predicts emotion intensity “without leveraging on lexicons.”
The baseline was the Weka baseline.

They report that EmoAtt outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but underperformed it on the test set. In Table 2, EmoAtt achieved average Pearson correlations of 0.689 on development and 0.522 on test, versus the baseline’s 0.611 on development and 0.648 on test.

The dataset used was the training, validation, and test datasets provided for the EmoInt-2017 shared task, consisting of tweets annotated for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger. These tweets were annotated using Best-Worst Scaling (BWS).

They describe hate speech as promoting the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups, and note that it can incite hate crime. They also reference Twitter’s policy condemning the promotion of violence against people based on race, ethnicity, national origin, sexual orientation, gender identity, religious affiliation, age, disability, or serious disease.
English, French, and Arabic.
The aspects considered are the tweet’s directness, hostility type, target attribute, target group, and the annotator’s sentiment.
The final dataset contains 13,014 tweets in total: 5,647 English, 4,014 French, and 3,353 Arabic, plus a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language.

Semantic Web technologies present opportunities to improve machine translation by supporting semantic and syntactic disambiguation, named entity recognition and linking, handling non-standard speech such as slang and idioms through user-context and linked idiom datasets, and exploiting multiple Semantic Web resources and reasoning over ontologies. They also offer opportunities for translating and enriching knowledge bases across languages, generating bilingual ontologies or embeddings, and then incorporating these translated KBs into MT language models to improve translation quality, especially for entities and domain-specific expressions.

The challenges associated with using Semantic Web technologies in Machine Translation are that existing MT problems remain hard to solve, especially complex semantic ambiguity, structural divergence, and linguistic features such as tense and gender, and SWT have mostly been applied only at the semantic analysis step. In addition, SWT-based pre- and post-editing for disambiguation are tedious to implement for common words, applying reasoners for syntactic issues suffers from poor run-time performance, and low-resource or morphologically rich language pairs still lack sufficient bilingual data and research coverage.

Structural divergence, linguistic properties/features, excessive focus on English and European languages with poor research on low-resource pairs, limitations of SMT across domains, translating non-standard social-network speech texts, difficult translations among morphologically rich languages, and speech translation training data that differs widely from real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. These were extracted from 31 channels, giving 155 features, then reduced with KPCA and expanded with delta and delta-delta to a final EEG feature dimension of 90.

The baselines were traditional acoustic features, with the paper comparing recognition using only EEG features and using concatenated EEG plus acoustic features against acoustic-only speech recognition. It also compared using all 31 EEG sensors versus only sensors T7 and T8.

The dataset used was built by the authors using simultaneous speech-EEG recordings, where subjects spoke the first 30 sentences from the USC-TIMIT database. They created dataset A with 10 subjects in 40 dB background noise and dataset B with 8 subjects in 65 dB background noise.
No. In Table 3, all LadaBERT variants have lower accuracy than the BERT-Base teacher on all reported datasets.

Yes. They compare weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning in Section 4.3 and conclude in Section 4.5 that combining matrix factorization with weight pruning yields better gains than single methods.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
News articles from the DailyMail, i.e., news text.
They trained on two datasets created from DailyMail news. For evaluation, they used the DUC-2002 single-document summarization task and 500 articles from the DailyMail test set.
Yes. In the results, they include “NN-ABS” as a neural abstractive baseline and report that NN-WE consistently outperforms this purely abstractive model.

They focus on commonsense ontological knowledge, specifically hierarchical, transitive IS-A relations. The paper also contrasts this with non-hierarchical commonsense knowledge from raw text and mentions additional forms such as negation and other commonsense relations as future work.

They mean the embedding space is structured so that its geometry directly encodes ontology constraints: for order embeddings, a subcategory vector is greater than or equal to its supercategory vector in every coordinate. Thus predictions automatically obey the partial-order or lattice structure because the ordering is enforced in the representation space itself.
Yes. They evaluated pretrained sentiment models that had been trained on larger corpora, including a character-level model trained on 82 million Amazon reviews, without further adaptation.

The models extracted word unigrams and bigrams represented with TF-IDF, averaged 300-dimensional word2vec embeddings, and sentiment features from Empath lexical categories plus pre-trained sentiment embeddings from a biLSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank. They also experimented with GloVe and Sent2Vec representations.
Unanswerable
Grouping, connectivity, and discourse structure.
No. The AI2D annotations were created by crowd-sourced non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk, while AI2D-RST was annotated by trained experts.

They are compared through two experiments using graph neural networks on the same diagrams, evaluating how effectively the crowd-sourced AI2D graphs and expert-annotated AI2D-RST graphs represent diagram structure for classifying diagrams and their parts. The study reports node classification and graph classification experiments.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.
The trained experts are the five annotators who annotated the AI2D-RST subset of 1000 diagrams using the new multi-layer annotation schema.
They use both a Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) and a CNN model for controversy detection. The HAN uses bi-directional GRUs.

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset for baseline comparison, and for cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain experiments they generate a new dataset based on Wikipedia crawl data. The new dataset is seeded from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009.

Cross-domain proves to be the hardest setup. It has the lowest best F1 among the evaluated setups (HAN: 0.645), compared with cross-topic (0.753), while cross-temporal is described as comparatively stable and the across-annotators analysis is not framed as a harder setup.

They use a Wikipedia crawl dataset as weak-signal data: controversial seed pages from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” (2018 and 2009), expanded by crawling “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops. Negative seeds come from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint, and labels are assumed to propagate to linked pages.
Yes. They compare the neural semantic approaches to lexical baselines such as TfIdf-SVM and language models (LM-DBPedia, LM-wiki), and report these comparisons in the results.
The training data consisted of about 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2B tokens.

The videos were represented by 1500-dimensional frame-level visual feature vectors extracted from the video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks.
Yes. The document states that the default OpenNMT model used the “scaled Luong” attention mechanism, and it also discusses a Transformer model, which was used in Lample & Charton (2020).
An established neural machine translation architecture based on LSTMs with an attention mechanism is explored. Specifically, it uses the “scaled Luong” attention version.
Symbolic rewriting is the formal-to-formal task of learning to rewrite symbolic data. The paper investigates whether neural machine translation models can learn such rewriting.

They incorporate expert knowledge by using anchors: expert-selected words or variables are attached to latent factors and added to the objective through a mutual-information term between anchor variables \(X_i\) and latent factors \(Y_j\). This lets them seed topics and impose semantics—for example, anchoring a factor to the word “diabetes” so the model discovers other words associated with that concept.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge dataset.

No. The paper reports a preliminary evaluation showing Anchored CorEx produces more coherent and interpretable topics on two corpora, but the provided document does not mention any comparison against popular topic models such as LDA.
Unanswerable

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper’s integrated training model, F-Score Driven Model II, achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo service in China.
They used a modified labelled Weibo NER corpus for Chinese social media, as in Peng and Dredze (2016). They also used the same unlabeled text from the Sina Weibo service in China.
XNLI: Cross-lingual NLI; MLDoc: Document Classification; Cross-lingual Intent Classification; POS Tagging; Named Entity Recognition.

Yes. The paper states that MMTE outperforms mBERT on low-resource languages in zero-shot POS tagging, and in the analysis it says the gains are more pronounced in low-resource languages in the few-shot setting as well.
Yes. The paper mentions a “one model per language” setting and contrasts it with “one model for all languages,” implying that mBERT is fine-tuned separately for each language in that setting.
Unanswerable

They evaluate dialogue using both automatic and human evaluation. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses Likert scoring on fluency, engagingness, and consistency, and ACUTE-EVAL self-chat pairwise comparisons on engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.

They observe a significant gap between the cross-lingual model and other models. In human evaluation, their multilingual M-CausalBert achieves nearly a 100 percent winning rate against the cross-lingual XNLG model.

They compare against a translation-based Poly-encoder pipeline. It translates the target-language query into English, feeds it to the English Poly-encoder, and then translates the English response back into the target language: target query → English query → English response → target response.
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes. They automatically created their own contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus by extracting suitable examples and introducing contrastive translations.

The baselines are standard context-agnostic sentence-level models: for RNNs, a bidirectional RNN with attention trained with Nematus, and for Transformers, a standard Transformer-base. The paper also lists simple concatenation baselines, concat22 for both RNNs and Transformers, and concat21 for Transformers.

The experimented context-aware models were: recurrent concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2; and Transformer concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) source-context Transformer. These used additional context on the source side, target side, or both.
English and German.

They use a simple entity linking algorithm that directly links a mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links and estimates the probability of an entity given the mention string. They also use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result, and within the same document apply a heuristic to corefer generic person mentions to more specific mentions.

They use a fine-grained entity typing architecture that combines three representations: a context representation from two-layer BiLSTMs, a mention string representation from averaged word embeddings, and a KB type representation from entity linking. These are concatenated with the entity linking confidence score and fed into a three-layer MLP.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN.
1 domain expert.
F1-score is used for evaluation.
The fine-tuned BERT model gives an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset, using an 80–20 train-evaluation split.
Unanswerable

The labeling scheme is a BIO labeling scheme with 11 entity types: abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea. These represent abbreviations; groups/individuals; system concepts; operational concepts; generic SE terms; event-like information; organizations; artifacts/instruments; numerical values; location-like entities; and measures/features/behaviors, respectively.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed dataset contains roughly 3700 annotated sentences at the word-token level.
Precision, recall and F-measure.

Teufel et al. used sixteen types of hand-crafted features such as sentence position, sentence length, and tense; Widyantoro et al. used content features, qualifying adjectives, and meta-discourse features.

The paper uses word2vec-based embeddings: averaged word vectors learned by the classical word2vec algorithm, paragraph vectors (PV-DM, an extension of word2vec), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE). The reported embeddings include ACL+AZ embeddings with 100 and 300 dimensions, as well as embeddings trained on MixedAbs and Brown corpus.
No. They use existing annotated data: the test dataset is the Argumentative Zoning Corpus, which consists of 80 AZ-annotated conference articles.

Sentence embeddings are generated using three explored models: by averaging the word2vec word vectors of all words in a sentence, by training paragraph vectors with the PV-DM (paragraph vector) model, and by training BAS-specific word embeddings and then averaging those word vectors in the sentence. The averaging model computes a sentence vector as the mean of the embeddings of its words.

Argumentative zoning, also called rhetorical sentence classification, is the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences. It provides readers with general discourse context so scientific ideas can be better linked, compared, and analyzed.

They crawled two blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts on them, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted for gaining retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects and also collected the authors’ timelines to obtain genuine tweets by the same users.

They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods and feature-concatenation classifiers. Specifically, the baselines are Spam Detection 1 (Wu et al. [5]), Spam Detection 2 (Rajdev et al. [12]), and Feature Concatenation with Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English
They look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically three categories: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.
English and Chinese for the two-language pretraining, and English, French, and Chinese for the three-language pretraining.

The decoder is a Transformer decoder in a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model, conditioned on the encoder’s vectors and a specific language tag to generate the target-language output sequence. In the reported setup, the model uses a 6-layer decoder with 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations in each Transformer layer.
The encoder is a 10-layer Transformer encoder with 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations in each layer.

Their baselines are COREfNQG, MP-GSN, and XLM for supervised English-English question generation; and for zero-shot cross-lingual question generation they use XLM and pipeline baselines based on XLM, including versions with translation and Google Translator.

The human evaluation metrics are a triple pairing task, where judges select the correct prompt-story pairing among shuffled groups of three stories, and a blind human preference comparison evaluating hierarchical generation versus generation without a prompt. They report the percentage of human preference for each model in this comparison.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

Language-model baselines: non-hierarchical story generators that do not condition on the prompt, using a gated convolutional language model (GCNN) and an additional self-attention mechanism. Seq2seq baselines: LSTM and convolutional seq2seq architectures, including Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention, plus an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models. They also compare against a KNN baseline that retrieves the closest training prompt using TF-IDF vectors created with FastText and FAISS.
No. The paper mentions a pretrained seq2seq model and a pretrained language model in its fusion approach, but it does not mention using pre-trained embeddings like BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017).
The dataset consists of stories scraped from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, where users submit story premises and other users respond with story responses. Unanswerable
The stories are collected from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, scraped over three years using the official Reddit API.
The experimented pre-trained embeddings were word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

SICK, STSB, and MRPC (Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus) were used. SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also for semantic textual similarity, STSB for semantic textual similarity, and MRPC for paraphrase identification.
They compare with DT TEAM, ECNU, BIT, TF-KLD, Multi-Perspective CNN, InferSent, GRAN, and Paragram-Phrase. They also include HCTI in the overall evaluation table.
Fleiss’s Kappa.

Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement in Table 3 using Fleiss’s Kappa, with values such as 0.23 for aggression, 0.18 for repetition, 0.42 for harmful intent, 0.51 for visibility among peers, and 0.18 for cyberbullying.
170 workers.

The social-network features are five neighborhood-overlap measures between author and target in the directed following network: downward overlap, upward overlap, inward overlap, outward overlap, and bidirectional overlap. They also use basic user-account metrics: each user’s number of friends and followers, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
The five factors are aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance.

Cyberbullying is defined in the document using the criteria of aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. It is also discussed as an extension of traditional bullying, which Olweus defines through repetition, harmful intent, and an imbalance of power.

The output was evaluated for similarity to the training data using Phrase Overlap match and K-gram-Jaccard similarity, for syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser, and for quality/humor content using human judgment. Syntactic accuracy was measured as the percentage of sentences with at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.
They use filters of size 1 × 3 in the convolution layer.

On FB15k-237, CapsE outperforms the previous state-of-the-art ConvKB by 0.105 MRR points (about 25.1% relative improvement) and 6.1% absolute Hits@10. On WN18RR, it outperforms previous state-of-the-art models only on MR, achieving the best MR score.
No, the experiments use only BERT; the paper states that under the finetune-only strategy, they use only a single BERT model.
They compared BERT with BERT + Bi-LSTM, BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, an ensembled model, BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM with the first bi-LSTM removed, and BERT + Sim-Transformer.
CoNLL03, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset, and Quora-Question-Pair.

They report raw agreement/kappa averaged over three pairwise comparisons as follows: Phase 1—scene .92/.90, function .95/.93, construal .90/.88; Phase 2—scene .93/.92, function .90/.88, construal .89/.88.

They annotated the Mandarin translation of The Little Prince manually: after Jieba tokenization, they manually corrected segmentation so potential adpositions were separate tokens, then all annotators jointly identified adposition targets according to their criteria, and supersense labels were added with POS tags and dependency trees stored in CoNLL-U-Lex format. The corpus was annotated by three native Mandarin speakers; 25% of adposition targets were labeled cooperatively by all three, while the rest were labeled independently and then adjudicated cooperatively in two phases.
The final corpus includes all 27 chapters of The Little Prince, with a total of 20k tokens.

They use a training dataset with five features: ID, title, author, text, and label, containing about 20,800 samples. They do not use the provided unlabeled test dataset; instead, they randomly select a test set from the training data for evaluation.

The paper explores three text representation models—TF-IDF, CountVectorizer, and Word2Vec—and five fine-tuning models: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM).

The essays are represented with six feature groups: numerical features; probabilities under two trigram language models; word-cluster features from k-means clusters of ConceptNet embeddings; topic-model features from LDA topic distributions; part-of-speech tag unigrams encoded as bag-of-words; and essay-text bigram bag-of-words with idf weighting. Numerical features include counts such as sentences, words, letters, Gunning Fog index, difficult words, misspelled words, duplicate words, and words with below-average idf.
The evaluation metrics were the challenge’s official error measure E and accuracy. The paper also reports a confusion matrix for 3-fold stratified cross-validation predictions.
Gradient boosted trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in Microsoft’s Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), are used as the classification model.

Future work is to investigate possible biases in the system, especially whether the usefulness of LDA topic-distribution features reflects true modeling power or merely an artifact of the dataset and information leakage from essay subjects tied to candidate level. The author suggests further analysis and validation, specifically using released essay topics to create validation splits based on topic.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for class imbalance. It achieved an error \(E\) of 10.10 and accuracy of 97.2.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated manually as text alignment quality using a 5-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence, 3 = partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information, 4 = correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information, and 5 = correct alignment and fully compositional translation. The corpus is split into three bins by hunalign confidence score, and two annotators each rate 30 items from each bin, with 10 shared items for inter-annotator reliability.

The speech alignment quality is evaluated by human annotators using a 3-point audio-text alignment scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with some words or sentences possibly missing, and 3 = correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at the start or end. Two annotators each rated 30 items from each of three bins.

Yes. The paper explicitly says the selective gate is designed to handle bags with only one sentence, where selective attention is ineffective, by dynamically assigning a low gating value to wrongly labeled instances.

Yes. The abstract states that selective attention can be incompetent when there is only one sentence in a bag, and that their proposed gating mechanism “performs stably and promisingly even if only one sentence appears in a bag”; however, the provided case study text does not explicitly say the shown examples are single-sentence bags.
Unanswerable
They evaluate on the standard SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge) dataset, using two tasks: semantic relatedness and textual entailment.

No. The experiments only report end-task performance on semantic relatedness and textual entailment, and the learned sentence representations are described as being passed to the next section to generate matching feature planes rather than being evaluated separately.
Unanswerable
The challenge used the NIST 2019 speaker recognition evaluation (SRE) CTS challenge dataset. The paper also reports results on the SRE18 development and SRE18 evaluation datasets.
The eftdnn subsystem outperformed the others.

No. They assume that enhancing frequencies of related words or phrases that co-occur in the same text increases their contribution to topics, but direct implementation did not reduce variation successfully and greatly degraded the unigram model until the assumptions were corrected.
Economic and political domains, and the Russian banking domain.
WordNet, EuroVoc, and RuThes.

They define hate speech as language that expresses hatred toward a targeted group or is intended to be derogatory, humiliating, or insulting to members of that group. In extreme cases, this may also include language that threatens or incites violence.
A logistic regression model with L2 regularization in a one-versus-rest framework.
33,458 Twitter users.
They extracted timelines for 33,458 Twitter users, yielding 85.4 million tweets, and then manually labeled a random sample of 24,802 tweets (from an initial 25k sample).
SST-2 is the task where fine-tuning all layers hurts performance, specifically for the large variants of BERT and RoBERTa.
Yes. They evaluate both base and large variants, and specifically mention that for the large models only 6 layers out of 24 for BERT and 7 for RoBERTa are sufficient.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

It mitigates the bias pattern by balancing hypothesis artifacts across labels: each training sample is reweighted by the inverse of its estimated label-given-hypothesis probability, using \(w = 1 / ((1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon)\), so training is equivalent to using an artifact-balanced distribution. In practice, \(P(y|h)\) is estimated with K-fold cross-prediction, and the label-wise sums of weights are normalized to keep the label prior unchanged.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK for cross-dataset testing. For mitigating annotation artifacts during evaluation, they train and validate on SNLI and MultiNLI and test on SICK and JOCI.
The entropy strategy was the most helpful. For both machine learning models (LR and RF), it reached optimal performance the quickest, while other strategies had no clear advantage or were not useful.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
10K sentences and 250K tokens.

The neural network models explored were a feedforward network with a context window and a bidirectional LSTM (biLSTM) for encoding, combined with either a softmax prediction layer with greedy decoding or a CRF layer with Viterbi decoding. They also experimented with word embeddings augmented by prefix, suffix, and character features, where character embeddings were composed using a max-pooled CNN.

The evaluated rule-based model was the rule-based tagger designed by Rashel et al. [15]. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted disambiguation rules.
The task uses the IDN Tagged Corpus proposed in [12], containing 10K manually tagged sentences and 250K tokens.
They use parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, resulting in 11,529,432 segmented words for training the embeddings.
No, they evaluate the embeddings only on word similarity and word analogy tasks.
Unanswerable

The paper identifies that the model lacked flexibility for out-of-vocabulary words, leading to much worse performance on an OOV test set. It also notes that unknown words were assigned a zero vector, which negatively affected testing results.
Yes. The evaluation reports results for “POS and morph classes” versus “Only POS classes,” including precision, recall, and F1 scores, so morphology detection is evaluated.
It extends ENAMEX by building a named entity recognition model based on the standard ENAMEX types: organization, location, and person.
Gender, number, and case.
Yes.

The dataset is a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets by more than 100,000 users who announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as bot, non-bot, or unavailable based on their public Twitter sites, with inter-annotator agreement of κ = 0.93 on 1,000 overlapping annotations. After resolving disagreements, 413 (4%) were bots, 7,849 (75.35%) were non-bots, and 2,069 (19.9%) were unavailable.

By evaluating Botometer on health-related user data and then extending it with a gradient boosting classifier that uses the Botometer score plus health-domain features. The added features were tweet diversity, URL score, mean and standard deviation of daily posts, topic heterogeneity from LDA, mean word length and its standard deviation, number of faces in the profile picture, and presence or absence of a person’s name in the user name; the system also used a 0.47 Botometer threshold and SMOTE to address class imbalance.

Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, and characterizing drug abuse. It also includes longitudinal user-level studies such as mining health-related information from cohorts of pregnant women.
They are from a casual conversational context.
bert-base-multilingual-cased
The dataset contains 2000 question-answer tuples.
They used a customer support dataset of 2000 conversation tuples collected by Hori and Hori, sourced from Twitter.

Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM) was used, described as fast gradient boosting on decision trees. They also used ensemble boosting by combining predictions from BERT and BioBERT base classifiers with TF-IDF and QIEF features.
Yes. They compared general BERT embeddings with domain-specific BioBERT embeddings, and found BioBERT performed better.
The improved dataset, PICONET, contains 96,328 sequences in total. This comes from 22,818 I, 7 IO, 337 IP, 10,994 O, 30,106 P, 13 PO, and 32,053 negative examples.

It was collected from PubMed structured abstracts by searching on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 English human clinical-trial abstracts, then lemmatizing section headings and carefully selecting heading labels that uniquely represented Population/Problem, Intervention, and Outcome. The authors manually inspected small samples of candidate labels, kept full abstract sections rather than splitting them into sentences, created multi-label examples for sections like “population and intervention,” added negative examples from headings such as “aim,” and cleaned the data with language identification, removal of missing values and unicode noise, and filtering to sequences of 5–200 words.
Unanswerable

Previous datasets used a keyword-based approach that produced ambiguous and redundant labels, because sections like “population and methods” were labeled as P even though they also contained intervention and study-design information. They also split multi-sentence sections into separate single-sentence examples, which the paper argues creates poorer candidates and loses feature-rich context.
Unanswerable

They explore four agreement relation types: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They examine these relations across the features number, gender, case, and person.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
TextFooler is the baseline their approach is compared to. They also evaluate across three text classification models: word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.
0.008 F1 points.
Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014). Their prior best model used scikit-learn’s Logistic Regression with 10-fold cross-validation and F1 tuning.
The baseline models are the All Sarcasm case, which assumes every instance is sarcastic, and the Random case, which randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic.
The explored domains are Twitter and Amazon product reviews.

The training data came from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. The Amazon reviews were from Filatova’s (2012) publicly available sarcasm corpus, and the Twitter data consisted of tweets collected in February–March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag: “#sarcasm” for sarcastic examples or one of “#happiness,” “#sadness,” “#anger,” “#surprise,” “#fear,” and “#disgust” for non-sarcastic examples.
LSTM and GRU with Attention was the best model, with 0.89 macro-F1. Its weighted average precision, recall, and F1 were 0.93, 0.88, and 0.88, respectively.

The models tested on the dataset were two SVMs (linear and RBF), Stochastic Gradient Descent Classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, Bernoulli Naïve Bayes, Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
LSTM and GRU with Attention best performs on the offensive language identification task. It achieved the highest macro-F1 among all models and is considered the best model trained for OGTD.
No. The annotations were done by a team of three volunteers, with disagreements resolved by one author and two extra human judges.
3 annotators.
No. The benchmark annotated dataset contained over 29% offensive content, with 1,401 offensive tweets and 3,378 not offensive tweets.

They experiment on five classical models and seven deep learning models. The classical models are linear SVM, RBF SVM, Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes; the deep learning models are Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
Unanswerable
910 reviews.
Three annotators.

It identifies the relationship between sentence-structure elements and word chunks using POS tags and a dependency-based parse tree. Program Π2 first maps words to the main sentence components—subject, verb, and object—based on the recognized sentence structure and Universal Dependency core arguments, while handling special “to be” cases separately; then Program Π3 recursively finds complement components from non-core and nominal dependency relations, such as compound nouns, adjective modifiers, conjunctions, prepositional phrases, and adverbial modifiers. The result is a list of vocabularies for the next steps.
The two use cases are NLG for annotated ontologies and using the system as an intermediate representation for wiki pages. These are described in Sections 4.1 and 4.2, respectively.

Yes. In Section 5.2, they explicitly explore the structure of the learned language vector space and show that the vectors preserve similarity properties between languages, using hierarchical clustering of 990 languages.
A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a final softmax output layer.

They show genetic relationships by hierarchically clustering the learned language vectors, as in Figure 5 for Germanic languages. The resulting clustering closely matches established genetic relationships in that language family.

No. They tested MagiCoder on about 4445 manually revised and validated ADR reports by automatically comparing human and MagiCoder encodings, while the claim that it reduces encoding time for pharmacologists is presented as a practical conclusion, not as a test on real pharmacologists.
Unanswerable

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: (1) definition of ad hoc data structures, mainly hash tables for efficient access to MedDRA terms and their words; (2) preprocessing of the original text, including tokenization, stemming, and removal of computationally irrelevant words; (3) a word-by-word linear scan with a voting task, where each word votes for the LLTs it belongs to and matching information is stored.  
Then it performs (4) weight calculation for recognized terms based on syntactical matching information, and (5) sorting/pruning of voted terms followed by release of the winning terms as the final solution.

The system is made linear by preprocessing the terminology into hash-table meta-dictionaries, DictByWord and DictByWordStem, that map each word or stem to the set of MedDRA terms containing it; building these structures takes O(km) and is done only when the dictionary changes. Then each narrative description is processed with a word-by-word linear scan and constant-time updates on auxiliary structures, so the construction is linear in the input size and in the terminology representation used for lookup.

The document does not report conclusions about the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder; it frames them as research questions. It only states that personal recovery in BD is important, that the CHIME model conceptualizes aspects as Connectedness, Hope and optimism, Identity, Meaning and purpose, and Empowerment, and that the authors expect broader, multilingual social-media analysis to yield new insights.
Unanswerable

The analysis was to use exploratory quantitative and mixed-methods approaches including statistical analysis and manual investigation, comparative corpus analysis with Wmatrix for keywords and key concepts, an iterative corpus-linguistic dictionary approach, semantic tagging with USAS, and distributional semantics. It also included unsupervised topic modelling via DLATK, as well as emotion and sentiment analysis using supervised machine-learning methods with multilingual embeddings.
No. The document states that they “will not ask the account holders of Twitter and Reddit posts included in our datasets for their consent.”

They will identify Twitter and Reddit users with bipolar disorder automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week,” extending the diagnosis patterns and terms for BD from Cohan et al. (2018). Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors contacted to obtain informed consent.
The training/testing data comes from CCPC1.0, a Chinese Classical Poetry Corpus consisting of 834,902 poems in total.
SHI and CI. Specifically, the generated major types are SHI forms Jueju and Lvshi, including Wuyan Jueju, Qiyan Jueju, Wuyan Lvshi, and Qiyan Lvshi, as well as 121 major forms (Cipai) of CI.

They compare their method with previous work on reducing OOV rates, specifically Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2015b) and Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). Their baselines use BPE and a fully character-based system implementing Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016).
Yes. They implement training with the word2vec tool and report settings for both SG and CBOW.

Quality of the word vectors is measured by evaluation tasks on the learned vectors: word similarity, synonym selection, and word analogy. In word similarity, they use the Pearson correlation coefficient between human similarity scores and cosine distances; in synonym selection, they choose the candidate with the highest cosine similarity to the stem word; in word analogy, they measure whether vector arithmetic with cosine similarity retrieves the correct fourth word.
No. They also report results on JPTimes, a secondary dataset collected from the Japan Times, in addition to KPTimes and other benchmark datasets.

They were collected by crawling selected online news websites, primarily The New York Times. The authors retrieved free-to-read New York Times article URLs from 2006 to 2017 and collected the corresponding archived HTML pages using the Internet Archive.
FirstPhrase, which extracts the first N keyphrase candidates from a document.

Two generative neural keyphrase generation models are trained: CopySci, trained on scientific abstracts (KP20k), and CopyNews, trained on newspaper articles (KPTimes). Both use the same CopyRNN encoder-decoder architecture with a copying mechanism.

Editors’ annotations are more uniform and consistent than those in existing datasets, because non-expert annotators use a larger, less controlled indexing vocabulary while editors use topic-descriptor-like keyphrases and tag suggestions. They are also more likely to generalize and assign missing keyphrases that do not occur in the document (about 55%), and their keyphrases are shorter on average in news datasets.
Unanswerable
Yes. They use neural word vectors learned through the unsupervised GloVe approach for the context words.
The model has 2 layers: one hidden layer and one classification layer.
F-measure is the evaluation metric they use, reported as F-measure(%). They also mention accuracy “in terms of F-measure.”
They used four datasets: DUC-2001, Inspec, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
27.53 on DUC, 27.01 on Inspec, 4.30 on ICSI, and 9.10 on NUS.
The document compares its system against SingleRank and Topical PageRank. Among these, “Our System” achieves the best results on all four corpora, outperforming SingleRank and Topical PageRank.

They do not directly verify gold word alignments; instead, they treat the ART teacher model’s encoder-decoder attention distributions as word alignment information. They justify this by observing that good attentions are usually considered to lead to accurate translation, and they train the NART student to match the teacher’s per-head attention distributions via KL-divergence so it has similar word alignments.

The ART Transformer baseline has a latency of 784 ms. The proposed NART model is 30.2× faster without rescoring and 17.8× faster with teacher rescoring, implying the unparallelizable ART model is about 30.2 times slower than NART in the single-sentence setting.
BLEU score is used to measure translation accuracy.
Yes. Besides WMT14, the model was also evaluated on the IWSLT14 German-to-English dataset.
Yes. The experiments report results on WMT14 English-to-German, WMT14 German-to-English, and IWSLT14 German-to-English, so they are not limited to only German-English.

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with the highest information gain obtained by average-based features. Specifically, the top features listed are PE-avg, AR-avg, MX-max, PE-max, ES-min, and ES-avg, while probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate dataset (the DSLCC development set), LDR, Skip-gram, and SenVec obtained comparable results, supporting the robustness of LDR. LDR achieved: Bulgarian 99.9, Macedonian 99.9, Spain Spanish 84.7, Argentina Spanish 88.0, Portugal Portuguese 87.4, Brazilian Portuguese 90.0, Bosnian 78.0, Croatian 85.8, Serbian 86.4, Indonesian 99.4, Malay 99.2, Czech 99.8, Slovak 99.3, and Other languages 99.9.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

It is evaluated on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset for script event prediction, where given an existing event context, the system chooses the most reasonable subsequent event from a candidate list. The paper follows Li et al. (2018a) and uses the SGNN framework, replacing its input event embeddings with the proposed intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses four datasets: the hard similarity dataset from Weber et al. (2018) with 230 event pairs plus an extended 1,000-pair version annotated by the authors, the transitive sentence similarity dataset from Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh (2014) with 108 sentence pairs, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset from Granroth-Wilding and Clark (2016) for script event prediction. For stock market prediction, the document does not specify a dataset name.
No. In the experiments, they compare against SGNS, PPMI-SVD, and a Char2Vec variant without attention, but not against models like fastText that include subword information.

Yes. The paper only reports English results, but states that English is morphologically impoverished and that the model performs better on words with richer morphology, so it is expected to perform even better for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.
English; they also expect it to perform even better for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.

The embeddings are evaluated by ranking word pairs according to the cosine similarity of their word vectors and then measuring the correlation with human similarity judgments using Spearman’s ρ. This comparison is done on datasets where annotators rate word-pair similarity on a fixed scale.
15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, NR (Neural Reasoner), and AR (Attentive Reader). Addition and Addition-proj are simpler distributed-representation baselines, while NR and AR are prior top-performing deep neural QA models.
The baseline is a one-stage RNN system. Its RNN structure is the same as the last stage, containing a 2-layer BLSTM, and it is directly trained to recognize dialect category.

The best result is achieved by the two-stage BLSTM system with 88.88% accuracy overall, 87.72% on utterances ≤3s, and 90.04% on utterances >3s, compared with the baseline’s 78.85%, 77.60%, and 80.10%. Both multi-stage systems improve relative accuracy by about 10% over the baseline, and the two-stage system performs best and is more robust on short-duration speech.
Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
Transformer architecture.
Unanswerable
The restricted system does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets.
Building Educational Applications
There is no clear winner in human evaluation. MODULAR is better at reducing bias, while CONCURRENT better preserves meaning and fluency.
The edits were made by English Wikipedia editors, i.e. a Wiki community effort. The paper says the corpus consists of sentences “pre and post-neutralization by English Wikipedia editors.”

Subjective text is automatically neutralized as a source-to-target generation task: given a subjective sentence, the system produces an output with similar meaning but with subjective bias removed. The paper proposes two encoder-decoder approaches: MODULAR, which first uses a BERT-based classifier to identify subjective words and then edits the sentence via an LSTM decoder using a join embedding to modify hidden states of flagged words, and CONCURRENT, which uses a BERT encoder and directly generates a neutralized sentence during decoding.
The investigated task is American Sign Language recognition at the word level, specifically recognizing 51 ASL word classes using multimodal RGB video and skeletal data.
The best model is Spatial AI-LSTM with 81% average cross-subject accuracy.
